texts.text: The Foundational Principle of Finance\nA dollar today is worth more than a dollar tomorrow.

Create a cash flow time line.
Compute the future value of money.
Show how the power of compound interest increases wealth.
Calculate the present value of a payment made in the future.

Move cash flows from one year to another.
Apply the Rule of 72.
Compute the rate of return realized on selling an investment.
Calculate the number of years needed to grow an investment.
The Four Variables Driving TVM Calculations
APR= Annual Percentage Rate (also known as interest rate)
Time Value of Money: Future Value of Lump Sum
How much will you have earned at the end of 1 year?
Time Value of Money: Future Value of Lump Sum
How much will you have earned 1 year in the future (FV)?
Future Value (FV) at the end of One Year

Present Value (PV) = $100 to be invested today
Time frame (n) =  1 year
Interest Rate (i)=  5%
FV= $100 *(1+.05)^1
FV= $100*(1.05)
FV= $105
The Excel Sign Convention:\nThink Like a Wallet
Excel requires you to define the direction of the cash flow.

Rate: The interest rate per period. (e.g., 5% or 0.05)
Nper: The total number of payment periods. (e.g., 3
for 3 years)
Pmt: The payment made each period. For a single
lump-sum investment, this is 0.
PV: The present value, or initial investment.
Crucially, this is entered as a negative number
because it's a cash outflow. (e.g., -100)
Type: [Optional] 0 for end-of-period payments, 1 for\nbeginning. Omit for this type of problem.
If I invest $100 today at 5% interest, what will it be worth in 3 years?
Key Insight: You earn "interest on interest." This is the power of compounding.

Open up Excel
Create a TVM file – you will use it for homework problems, quizzes and exams
Please save it with your name in the file
Example:
Beth.TVM-File.210
In Class Exercise #1 – What is the FV in year “n”?
Build a calculator that allows for changing units

Build and Graph a table for compounding wealth over time
Across 10 years (n)
3 different interest rates(i)  5% , 10% and 15%
Graph the future values (FV) of $1,000 PV
Future Value: The Power of\nInterest on Interest
Simple Interest: Earning only on the principal
Compound Interest: Earning on the\nprincipal + accumulated interest
Simple Interest = $$Amt * Interest Rate * n periods of time

Future Value – Present Value = Total Interest Paid
$122.50 -$100 = $22.50
Interest on Interest = Total Interest Paid - Simple Interest
$22.50-$21 = $1.50
Calculate the Simple Interest earned on the following scenario:
$1,500 investment today, deposited for 3 year in the bank at an interest rate of 7.00%
Two Sides of the Same Coin
Discounting is simply Compounding in reverse.
Present Value: What is the\nFuture Worth Today?
Negative because\nthis is the amount you\nmust invest today.

PV(rate, nper, pmt, [fv], [type])
• Rate: The discount rate per period. (e.g., 6% or
0.06)
• Nper: The total number of periods. (e.g., 5 for 5
years)
• Pmt: The payment made each period. Use 0 for
a single future lump sum.
• FV: The future value you are discounting back
• Type: [Optional] Omit for this type of problem.
Example: =PV (6%, 5, 0, 10000) returns -$7,472.58. The negative sign signifies that this is the cash\noutflow you would need to invest today to get the positive future value.
Practice Scenario: The AB Tools Case\nHow much do you need to deposit today to have $50,000 in 5 years?
Interpretation: You must ‘lock away' (outflow) ~$37,362.91 today to reach your goal.
How do your functions change with three different interest rates?
Compunding with Multiple Rates - Future Value
Suppose we expect interest rates to increase over the next few years, from 7% this year, to 8% next year, to 8.5% in the third year\nWhat is the future value of a future $1,500 cash flow in 3 years?
Discounting with Multiple Rates - Present Value
Suppose we expect interest rates to increase over the next few years, from 7% this year, to 8% next year, to 8.5% in the third year\nWhat is the present value of a future $2,500 cash flow in year 3?
Scenario: What is the Present Value of $2,500 received in 3 years if discount rates are 7%, 8%, and 8.5%?
Negative PV cashflow is handled in the equation
Add a Rates tab to your TVM Workbook
Twelve years ago, you invested $4,800.\nToday, your investment is worth $8,750.\nWhat is your rate of return?
What Was Your Rate of Return?
Twelve years ago, you invested $4,800. Today, that investment is worth $8,750.\nWhat annual rate of return (i) did you earn?
Note the PV is negative (outflow) and FV is positive (inflow).
Information needed to solve for the number of years that you will need to accumulate money.
Starting cash flow.\nInterest rate.\nFuture cash flow (or the amount you will need).
Complex calculation – use financial calculator and Excel!
Solving for Time – give it a try..
When interest rates are 9%, how long will it take $5,000 to double?
Solving for Time Example with Excel

The True Power: Moving Any Cash Flow Through Time\nFuture Value and Present Value are not just destinations; they are directions. You can use\nthese principles to find the equivalent value of a cash flow at any point on the timeline.

Moving it Later (Compounding):
What is its value in Year 10?
We move it forward 5 years:
FV = $1,000 * (1.08)^5 = $1,469.33

Moving it Earlier (Discounting):
What is its value in Year 2?
We move it backward 3 years:
PV = $1,000 / (1.08)^3 = $793.83
What if we could build a “Time Machine”??
What if we could build a “Time Machine”??
To make your "Time Machine" work, you need a formula that automatically detects if you are moving Forward (requiring an FV function) or Backward (requiring a PV function).

Think of an IF statement in Excel as a simple logic tool that asks a question: "Is this specific condition true?"
Depending on the answer, Excel will perform one of two different actions.
The Anatomy of an IF Statement
The formula is broken down into three parts, separated by commas:
=IF(Your Question, What to do if Yes, What to do if No)
The Question (Logical Test): This is where you compare two things.
For example, is one year further in the future than another?  Target Year > Initial Year
Value IF True: This is the result or calculation Excel performs if your question is answered with a "Yes."
Performs a FV calculation
Value IF False: This is what Excel does if the answer is "No."
Performs a PV calculation
